title: Breaking the width-scaling limit in high-performance atomically thin 2D nanoribbon transistors
authors: Sameer Kumar Mallik, Adrian Christiansen, Saroj P. Dash
abstract: State-of-the-art transistors have been successfully scaled the gate lengths and channel thicknesses down to 5 nm for high-performance and energy-efficient information processing. However, reducing channel width below 40-50 nm remains a bottleneck, as dangling bonds, edge disorder, and lateral depletion suppress drive current and degrade device performance. Here, we break this width-scaling wall using ultra-scaled two-dimensional semiconductor (2DSC) nanoribbon transistors down to 15 nm. In contrast to the conventional scaling rule of degradation of current density upon width scaling, our atomically-thin monolayer and bilayer molybdenum disulfide nanoribbon transistors exhibit enhancement of on-current density of up to 230% and 170%,respectively, followed by a saturation for the narrowest channels down to 15 nm. The ultra-narrow nanoribbon transistors maintain the highest on/off ratios reported so far for similar device dimensions, with improved mobility and threshold-voltage stability, indicating reduced edge scattering and depletion with a stronger electrostatic control. These findings lead to a breakthrough in width scaling rules using 2DSC nanoribbons with enhanced performance at narrower channel widths, which is promising for the ultimate scaling of transistors.
summary: This paper reports a breakthrough in scaling high-performance transistors by utilizing atomically thin MoS2 nanoribbons down to 15 nm. Contrary to expected performance degradation, the devices show an enhancement in on-current density, suggesting an 'edge-enhanced transport regime.' This work establishes a new scaling rule for 2D semiconductors, crucial for future ultra-low-power electronics.
main_problem: The primary challenge addressed is overcoming the 'width-scaling wall' in transistor fabrication, where reducing channel width below 40-50 nm typically degrades performance due to edge disorder and depletion.
main_result: The study demonstrates that using atomically thin 2D semiconductor (2DSC) nanoribbons, specifically MoS2, allows for an enhancement of on-current density as the width is scaled down to 15 nm, establishing a new scaling rule.
method: The research involves fabricating and electrically characterizing MoS2 nanoribbon transistors using advanced top-down lithography and etching techniques.
model_or_system: The physical system is the atomically thin MoS2 nanoribbon transistor, tested in both monolayer and bilayer forms, fabricated on standard semiconductor substrates.
key_observables: On-current density (I_D), on/off ratios, field-effect mobility (mu_FE), and threshold voltage (V_TH) as functions of channel width.
important_parameters: Channel width (W_CH) scaled down to 15 nm; the transition from an edge-enhanced transport regime to a saturation regime.
main_assumptions: The performance enhancement is attributed to an 'edge-enhanced transport regime' that mitigates traditional scaling limitations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the fabrication process, electrical transfer/output characteristics across various widths (e.g., 50 nm down to 15 nm), and comparative plots showing current density enhancement and mobility trends.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first identifying the scaling bottleneck, presenting the fabrication methodology for 2DSC nanoribbons, and then systematically presenting electrical characterization data showing current enhancement and saturation at the narrowest widths.
why_interesting: While focused on solid-state electronics, the concept of achieving enhanced performance through nanoscale confinement and edge effects relates to understanding boundary conditions and quantum confinement in low-dimensional systems, which has parallels in quantum physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Width scaling wall in advanced transistors and opportunity for 2D semiconductor nanoribbons. (a) Historical technology trends for channel width 𝑊 (top) and channel length 𝐿 (bottom). Nanosheet transistors show continuous 𝐿 scaling down to a few nanometres, whereas 𝑊 has saturated around ∼40-50 nm showing a practical “width-scaling wall” due to all-surface scattering at narrow widths. (b) Conceptual schematics of on-current density 𝐼ON versus channel width 𝑊 for Si nanoribbons (cyan) and 2DSC nanoribbons (magenta) highlighting an edge-enhanced regime (shaded) in which only lateral edge states contribute to scattering in 2DSC.
